title: Lower inflation, weaker activity: what foreign import tariffs mean for the euro area
summary: ECB staff researchers analyze how US import tariffs affect the euro area using a novel "tariff-related trade surprise" (TTS) methodology. The study finds that when US tariffs rise and euro area exports to the US fall unexpectedly, both euro area inflation and economic activity decline over the medium term, resembling an adverse demand shock. The demand-dampening effects of lower export volumes outweigh any inflation-boosting supply effects from higher global input costs.
key_points: Authors use a two-step TTS methodology: gravity model residuals combined with US tariff change data to identify trade shocks plausibly caused by tariffs (2002-2019 pre-pandemic data, 20 euro area countries, 192 trading partners)
Medium-term impact: a 1% trough decline in bilateral euro area exports to the US leads to approximately 0.1% lower consumer price level about 1.5 years after the TTS

Downstream sectors (final goods like machinery, autos, pharmaceuticals): output declines ~0.3% and producer prices fall ~0.1% one year after TTS; shock transmits over 1-2 years through production chains
Upstream sectors (intermediate inputs like chemicals): react more immediately since they operate at the beginning of value chains
Monetary policy can help counter adverse effects because sectors most exposed to TTSs are also most responsive to interest rate changes
Local projections methodology used with controls including industrial production, HICP, unemployment rate, EUR/USD exchange rate, sovereign bond yields, and commodity prices
why_it_matters: This ECB research quantifies how external tariff shocks transmit to the euro area economy, providing policymakers and investors with empirical estimates of disinflationary and growth-dampening effects. The finding that demand effects dominate supply effects suggests tariff wars could provide more room for ECB easing rather than forcing tighter policy to combat imported inflation.
macro_context: External tariff shocks represent a disinflationary force for the euro area over the medium term (12-18 month horizon). The 0.1% CPI decline per 1% export shock is modest but meaningful for a bloc already near its 2% inflation target. The ECB could face a scenario where trading partner tariffs simultaneously weaken growth and lower inflation, potentially giving more flexibility for rate cuts. EUR/USD exchange rate movements are noted as a transmission channel, with higher US tariffs potentially affecting relative interest rate expectations.
notes: Source is a substantive ECB staff research blog post with novel methodology. Published 50 days before TODAY (2026-04-01), well within the 90-day freshness window. The article is directly relevant to macro catalysts as it analyzes an external shock (US tariffs) and its transmission to the euro area. No direct market impact projection is made in the source; confidence set to 0 for market_impact as this is research commentary rather than a policy announcement.
